next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Low tide gave the route back a little at a time, as if the mangroves were reconsidering a bad decision. First the blackwater slipped off the prop roots and left them shining to their elbows. Then the surface broke into tannin puddles and narrow runs that showed every trapped mullet and drowned leaf. By early afternoon the mud path under the camp was visible in sections, a dark braid through the roots toward the old concrete mouth of the BrightCurrent intake. Mina stood on the screened walkway outside the kitchen with the taste of bad coffee gone metallic in her mouth and watched the path appear.

She had spent the morning pretending to help Beto inventory lantern batteries and cracked dock lines, his burn-marked wrist flashing every time he turned the clipboard over to study the anatomy terms he kept copying there for trade-school prep. Imogen took a donor call in the office with one silver hairpin already working loose from her bun and spoke in that careful bright voice she used when money was somewhere on the line. Tomas, who usually came up by noon smelling of two-stroke and mangrove mud, had not come up from the county dock. Suspended men did not have much reason to visit other people who had helped suspend them. Rhea had gone to the bat lab with her braid still damp at the nape from washing guano out of it and shut the door with the soft finality that meant work, or distance, or both. The camp creaked in the heat. Screens tapped. Somewhere under the stilts a spoonbill chick made the rusty hinge noise they made when hungry enough to sound furious.

If she...
open_hooks_to_carry: Tomas is pulled off the night route because the fuel numbers no longer hide.
Rhea has to decide whether to protect her marriage, her housing, or Mina's obsession.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...A  
EQUIPMENT / COUNTY ACCESS - TOMAS  
OBSERVATION MEMO - MINA

At the bottom she had written, underlined twice, NO OUTSIDE CALLS WITHOUT CLEARANCE.

Mina stared at that last line. It should have felt insulting. Instead it felt like the chapter closing on one kind of ownership and opening on another, colder one. She thought of the empty sponsor field on the permit portal, of Seth Haviland saying respectable, of the compliance man's careful voice around the phrase not already under sanction. Proof came with people, Rhea had said. She had not added that people came with ledgers.

"Fine," Mina said.

Imogen nodded once, as if agreement were merely one more signature acquired. She reached for the camp letterhead and began feeding a sheet into the printer. The machine whined, caught, then spat the page out crooked. From the kitchen Beto shouted, "Kick it again," without needing to be asked.

Tomas laughed, finally real. Rhea rolled her eyes and straightened the sheet. Mina stood there with the task list in her damp hand while the office filled with printer noise, spoonbill screams, frying onions, and the low electrical tick of county gear cooling on an island that could not afford honesty except in pieces.

By the time the letterhead printed clean, the eel had acquired a sponsor, a witness log, a borrowed camera, and four people who could lose money if it disappeared. It was still out there in blackwater under roots and bird shit and heat shimmer. But it no longer belonged to darkness alone. It belonged to signatures, to debt, to whatever happened when a miracle crossed a desk.
state_deltas_required: Fuel audit, lost access to night channels, and money problems made newly visible
Tomas is suspended, the triangle splits along class and blame lines, and Mina loses easy access to the creek.
